primary_reason: The study applies collaborative benchmarking to azithromycin use for respiratory infections during a national Mycoplasma pneumoniae surge, offering a transferable antimicrobial-use surveillance design.
The microsimulation estimates clinical and cost-effectiveness thresholds for long-acting tuberculosis treatment.
The study applies the validated WHO point-prevalence survey and AWaRe classification to benchmark hospital antibiotic prescribing and identify stewardship opportunities.

The study benchmarks seven minority-variant callers on simulated Mycobacterium tuberculosis sequencing data, validates their ranking with strain mixtures, and develops a new false-positive error filter.
The study integrates meteorological drivers and antigenic variation into a SIRS model and evaluates their contribution to multiyear influenza simulation and forecasting.
The systematic review and meta-analysis synthesizes randomized trials using laboratory-confirmed outcomes to benchmark direct and indirect influenza vaccine effectiveness.
The study evaluates chelation of amyloid-beta aggregates in Alzheimer's disease and supplies no concrete transfer route to infectious-disease epidemiology.
The simulation diagnoses severe non-inferiority bias caused by imperfect tuberculosis infection outcome measurement.
The study benchmarks multiple large language models and prompting strategies against manually labeled abstracts for extracting pathogen–post-acute-syndrome associations.
The study develops survey-derived lifetime sexual-activity strata and demonstrates their calibration and validation within an agent-based HPV transmission model.
The study develops Gaussian-process emulation and Bayesian history matching to calibrate a computationally intensive SARS-CoV-2 agent-based model against age-specific hospitalization data.
The review benchmarks antigen-identification models for vaccine and immunotherapeutic design rather than epidemiology.
The study incorporates real-time healthcare-worker and equipment movement data into an agent-based Clostridioides difficile transmission model to assess infection-control interventions.
The benchmark evaluates uncertainty in therapeutic antigen design models rather than epidemiological inference.
The study calibrates an agent-based HIV transmission model to longitudinal cohort data and uses counterfactual scenarios to separate biomedical and behavioral drivers of incidence decline.

The study combines high-fidelity care simulations, genetically distinguishable phage surrogates, and video-coded contacts to reconstruct potential multidrug-resistant-organism transmission pathways in nursing homes.

The study operationalizes supportive-care guidelines as structured EHR queries, validates them across two sites, and measures adherence for actions including antifungal prophylaxis and Clostridioides difficile treatment.

The prospective cohort uses PET-CT as a sensitive reference benchmark, symptom-agnostic follow-up, and time-to-event analysis to evaluate asymptomatic tuberculosis progression and chest-radiograph CAD performance.
The physician survey reports treatment preferences and thresholds without developing an epidemiological decision method.
The calibrated multi-host mechanistic model estimates interspecific transmission and counterfactual control effects.
The agent-based study compares influenza-vaccination campaign channels, personalization, budgets, and framing under explicit uptake and equity guardrails.

The study characterizes antibacterial phytochemicals through susceptibility testing, network pharmacology, docking, and molecular dynamics but does not contribute a transferable epidemiologic design, model, or population-measurement strategy.
The study combines computational fluid dynamics, quantitative microbial risk assessment, and pathogen-specific dose-response curves to estimate respiratory-virus transmission risk in shared bedrooms.
The study develops, externally validates, and benchmarks an interpretable infectious-disease severity score.
The study benchmarks histopathology models for endometrial cancer subtyping and provides no concrete infectious-disease transfer route.
The study descriptively compares human and livestock Q fever surveillance patterns across regions without identifying a reusable analytic, adjustment, or transmission-model contribution.
The mechanistic model concerns inhibitor-induced hormesis in neurodegenerative protein aggregation and states no concrete route to infectious-disease epidemiology.
The study benchmarks multimodal models against specialists for infectious-disease diagnosis, treatment, and urgency triage.
The study uses a multistate population-level HIV transmission model to project infections under permanent and temporary disruptions of Ryan White services.
The study constructs an agent-based representation of conference activities and close contacts to estimate short-duration infectious transmission and compare control strategies.

The study combines a systematic review, simulations, and trial-data reanalysis to compare two-step regression with mixed models for estimating early fungicidal activity in cryptococcal-meningitis trials.
The study compares laboratory antimicrobial-surface assays without developing a transferable epidemiological method or infectious-process model.

The study compares agent-based Taenia solium model configurations incorporating age-dependent susceptibility, immunity, and GPS-derived exposure heterogeneity and validates them against village prevalence and cyst-burden data.

The title indicates an updated comparison of language models for antibiotic prescribing, but the absent abstract leaves the benchmark design, validation data, and substantive method content unresolved.

The study establishes a follow-up MRI recommendation benchmark for shoulder ultrasound, and the isolated mention of post-vaccination pain does not provide a concrete infectious-disease transfer route.
The study characterizes structural and genetic features of hepatitis C neutralizing antibodies without contributing a transferable epidemiologic design, population model, or measurement framework.
The study validates genetic prevalence estimates for inherited congenital disorders against newborn screening data but supplies no concrete transfer route to infectious-disease outcomes.

The study attempts to reproduce nine published Pseudomonas aeruginosa LAMP assays, documents missing reporting details and performance discrepancies, and recommends stronger assay-reporting and quality standards.
The study combines systematic review, meta-analysis, and matching-adjusted indirect comparison to transport global long-acting HIV therapy trial results to a Southern Chinese cohort.
The study experimentally compares immune responses to candidate malaria vaccines but does not identify a transferable epidemiologic design, transmission model, or population-measurement contribution.
The study defines surgical safety benchmarks for sleeve gastrectomy, a non-infectious application with no stated route to infectious-disease epidemiology.
The study systematically benchmarks 13 domain-adaptation and transfer-learning methods across cross-site MALDI-TOF antimicrobial-resistance prediction scenarios and evaluates their label efficiency.
The study models environmental toxicant associations with antibody titers rather than an infectious epidemiological process or method.
The study benchmarks protein-language-model embeddings against mutation-based machine learning for HIV drug-resistance prediction with external, subtype, temporal, and interpretability evaluations.
The study reports genetic variants and docking simulations for a hereditary neurodegenerative disorder without a concrete infectious-disease transfer route.
The study develops a multicity extension of Covasim and uses controlled agent-based experiments, sensitivity analysis, and dynamic time warping to examine mobility effects and infer outbreak origins.
The study develops an agent-based cholera model for displacement camps and uses scenario analysis to separate domestic and public transmission under water-supply shocks.
The study applies standardized NSQIP surveillance, multivariable risk adjustment, and observed-to-expected benchmarking to track postoperative infectious morbidity over five years.
The study establishes a population reference for inherited variants and precision medicine but supplies no infectious-disease outcome or concrete transfer route to infectious-disease epidemiology.
The agent-based model addresses fibroblast senescence and pulmonary fibrosis without an infectious-disease outcome or a concrete infectious-disease transfer route.
The study uses a fitted enteric-transmission model as an independent reference for comparing three population-attributable-fraction estimators for norovirus and rotavirus diarrhea.
The study applies a reproducible pared-mean benchmarking design to multisite emergency-care measures that include infection management and antibiotic stewardship.
The study uses a calibrated multilayer contact-network simulation to identify how incidental contacts alter epidemic burden and the population groups driving SARS-CoV-2 transmission.
The review proposes a biological interpretation of post-infectious ME/CFS but does not develop, evaluate, or prescribe a transferable epidemiologic method.
The study simulates weather-dependent population dynamics of an infectious-disease mosquito vector with an agent-based framework.
The study creates an openly available annotated surgical-wound dataset, diagnostic benchmark, and staged model for assessing surgical-site infection risk and clinical urgency.
The study applies pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic modeling with Monte Carlo simulation to compare oritavancin dosing regimens for complex bacterial infections.
The study demonstrates a blinded multidimensional evaluation design for comparing the readability, quality, accuracy, and guideline concordance of pertussis education generated by language models.
The framework develops and benchmarks spatiotemporal forecasting of emerging pathogen-variant dominance.
The agent-based model evaluates opioid-use policy and mortality without an infectious-disease outcome or a concrete infectious-disease transfer route.
The calibrated agent-based model simulates chlamydia transmission and vaccination strategies in the United States population.

The study compares short- and long-read metagenomic pathogen detection using mock communities and clinical specimens while quantifying diagnostic accuracy, host-depletion effects, and turnaround trade-offs.
The mechanistic model concerns viral-vector manufacturing yield and quality rather than infectious disease and provides no concrete epidemiologic transfer route.
The simulation study evaluates the accuracy and sampling sensitivity of phylodynamic metrics intended to infer bridging between gonorrhea transmission networks.
The study uses measles-transmission simulations to compare quarantine durations and post-exposure vaccination policies in schools and daycares.
The study applies an agent-based transmission model with repeated runs and sensitivity analyses to estimate the impact of rotavirus vaccination in urban and rural Iranian children.
The study uses a calibrated age- and sex-structured chlamydia transmission model with economic simulation to compare national vaccination and catch-up strategies.

The study evaluates the climatic transferability of a mechanistic pathogen-forecasting model against six seasons of independent field observations, offering a validation design transferable to regional infectious-disease warning models.
The scoping review systematically compares how network and agent-based epidemic models represent contact structure when evaluating targeted vaccination strategies and identifies methodological gaps.
The study develops a quality-controlled multilingual vaccine benchmark and evaluates how model generation, prompting strategy, language, and vaccine type affect answer accuracy.

The study systematically benchmarks deep-learning architectures and preprocessing strategies for MALDI-TOF microbial identification, resistance prediction, and vector surveillance, including deployment trade-offs.
The study demonstrates a structured-observation instrument based on WHO standards and supplements measured infection-prevention adherence with facility-level validation discussions.
The agent-based model simulates hepatitis C cell-to-cell infection spread, proliferation, and focus merging.
The transferable mechanistic model predicts viral-vector bioprocess behavior and manufacturing timing but does not address infectious-disease epidemiology or specify a concrete transfer route to it.
The study benchmarks therapeutic protein candidates using molecular simulations and laboratory inhibition rather than epidemiological methods.
The simulated comparison evaluates two transferable decision protocols for neonatal sepsis management.
The benchmarking and guidance concern transposable-element RNA quantification without an infectious-disease outcome or a concrete infectious-disease transfer route.
The study validates agent-based contact assumptions against empirical survey matrices and quantifies how their discrepancies change simulated epidemic dynamics.
The systematic review uses random-effects evidence synthesis to compare treatment efficacy and severe-infection toxicity across frontline regimens for older adults with Hodgkin lymphoma.
The study develops and experimentally validates a three-dimensional agent-based model that generates erosion, sloughing, and shape change during Pseudomonas aeruginosa biofilm growth.
The study screens antimicrobial extracts and molecular binding properties for therapeutic development without epidemiological methodology.
The study reconstructs population pharmacokinetic models and uses Monte Carlo simulation to compare renal-function-stratified aztreonam-avibactam regimens for resistant bacterial infections.
The study demonstrates standardized multisector integration and benchmarking of national surveillance data across several healthcare-associated infections and multidrug-resistant organisms.

The study integrates structural indicators, clustering, regression, Monte Carlo uncertainty propagation, and time-series benchmarking to evaluate HPV-vaccination and health-system scenarios for cervical-cancer elimination.

The registry study primarily describes penetrating head injuries and uses standard regression for mortality, without developing a reusable infectious-outcome method despite listing hospital-acquired infection as a secondary outcome.
The study experimentally compares malaria-vaccine immunogens in mice but does not develop or evaluate a transferable epidemiologic model, measurement strategy, or evidence-synthesis method.
The study compares parametric and spline approaches for estimating time-varying vaccine efficacy and waning.
The study develops risk-standardized antibiotic-use metrics and compares their hospital benchmarking behavior against an existing metric.
The benchmark evaluates subcellular-localization prediction for human proteins without an infectious-disease outcome or a concrete infectious-disease transfer route.

The study primarily profiles immune abnormalities in non-tuberculous mycobacterial pulmonary disease, while its unvalidated random-forest marker model does not constitute a substantive transferable method contribution.
The retrospective surgical registry analysis establishes descriptive nasal-fracture outcomes with standard regression and reports no reusable infectious-outcome method.
The study benchmarks five perioperative risk tools by discrimination across outcomes and subgroups, including sepsis, surgical-site infection, and urinary tract infection.
The review concerns molecular protein-interaction prediction rather than transferable epidemiological inference or infectious-process modeling.

The study technically evaluates and validates an automated hospital-onset bloodstream-infection case-identification algorithm using national antimicrobial-resistance surveillance data and documents interpretation limitations.
The benchmark evaluates structural antigen-recognition models for biotherapeutic research rather than epidemiological analysis.
The study develops an antibacterial compound through molecular screening and preclinical experiments without epidemiological methods.
